In Iconium, the pattern emerges that will define this entire chapter: belief and opposition, often simultaneously. Paul and Barnabas preach with such power that many Jews and Greeks respond in faith—but unbelieving Jews immediately work to poison minds against them. What strikes us is that the apostles don't retreat or soften their message. Instead, they spend considerable time there, speaking boldly, while God Himself validates their words through signs and wonders. This is not the quiet persuasion of human rhetoric. This is the living God breaking into human experience and demanding a response. Yet even divine authentication divides rather than universally convinces. The city splits. Some follow the apostles; others align with their enemies. This should dismantle any naive hope that if we just present the gospel convincingly enough, resistance will crumble. The power of God's Word does not guarantee universal acceptance—it guarantees division between those who believe and those who oppose.

When persecution arrives in Lystra, we witness something extraordinary in the healing of the lame man. Paul perceives faith in this crippled beggar and commands him to stand. He walks. But notice what happens next: the crowds immediately reverse the categories. Instead of recognizing a miracle pointing to the God of Israel, they interpret it through their own mythology. Paul and Barnabas become Hermes and Zeus. Even a sign from God becomes an occasion for idolatry when people interpret it through their spiritual blindness. The apostles' response is violent—they tear their clothes in horror and rush into the crowd to redirect worship. Their message is blunt: we are men like you; turn from worthless things to the living God. They appeal to natural revelation, to the rain and seasons that testify to God's goodness. Yet even this direct confrontation barely stops the crowds from sacrificing. The human heart's capacity to misinterpret or resist divine reality is profound.

Then comes what should break us: the very Jews from earlier cities arrive and turn the crowd. They stone Paul and drag him outside the city, apparently dead. But he rises and returns to the city the next day. There is no explanation here, no theological commentary. Luke simply reports it. Paul's resilience is not superhuman strength but something deeper—a willingness to walk back into a hostile city that just tried to murder him. When he and Barnabas return through the cities they've visited, they tell their disciples plainly: "We must endure many hardships to enter the kingdom of God." This is their summary of the mission. Not triumph, not ease, but endurance through hardship as the normal Christian path. They appoint elders in each church, praying and fasting as they entrust these young believers to the Lord. Everything—the churches, the leaders, the future—goes into God's hands with intercession, not presumption.

When they return to Antioch to report, they celebrate what God has done and that He has opened the door of faith to the Gentiles. Yet this door has been opened at the cost of rejection, misunderstanding, violence, and the constant threat of death. The gospel's advance is real and it is miraculous, but it is never easy or triumphant in the way the world measures triumph. As you read this chapter, ask yourself: Am I prepared for the hardship that accompanies genuine faith? Do I expect resistance, or do I assume that doing God's work guarantees comfort? The apostles knew better. And they kept moving forward anyway.
